page: Amazon Web Services c1.xlarge is a first-generation compute-optimized server instance running on the Xen hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor operating at 1.8 GHz. The instance provides 8 dedicated physical cores and 8 vCPUs with a single thread per core. It is equipped with 7.0 GB of system memory, yielding a low memory-to-core ratio of 0.88 GB per core. Local storage is bundled, consisting of 1680 GB of HDD capacity, while network connectivity is limited to a 1 Gbps baseline.

Benchmark results indicate that the c1.xlarge delivers modest performance compared to modern standards. Single-core CPU performance in stress-ng and Geekbench is categorized in the bottom 10%, while multi-core performance falls in the bottom 25%. Memory bandwidth for large blocks is average, but small-block cached read bandwidth is weak. Cryptographic performance is supported by hardware AES acceleration, but the processor lacks modern instruction sets such as AVX, AVX-512, and AMX. Database operations in Passmark score in the average tier, whereas Redis SET operations perform poorly.

Given its legacy hardware profile, the c1.xlarge presents a specific tradeoff between high local HDD storage capacity and low computational density. The low memory allocation of 7.0 GB limits its utility for memory-intensive applications. This instance is best suited for legacy workloads, basic database operations that benefit from the large local HDD storage, or simple batch processing tasks that do not require modern vector or matrix instruction sets.
description: Amazon Web Services c1.xlarge is a legacy compute-optimized instance featuring 8 dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 cores running at 1.8 GHz on an x86_64 architecture. It is configured with 7.0 GB of system memory and a substantial 1680 GB of local HDD storage, operating on the Xen hypervisor with a 1 Gbps baseline network speed. Performance benchmarks place this instance in the lower tiers, with single-core and multi-core CPU metrics scoring in the bottom 10% and 25% respectively, though large-block memory bandwidth and database operations achieve average ratings. Lacking modern instruction sets like AVX-512, the c1.xlarge is best suited for legacy batch processing, basic database operations, and workloads requiring high local HDD capacity rather than high-performance compute.
og_description: Amazon Web Services c1.xlarge is a legacy compute-optimized instance with 8 Intel Xeon cores, 7.0 GB of RAM, and 1680 GB of local HDD storage, suitable for basic database and legacy batch workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services c1.xlarge is a legacy compute-optimized instance with 8 Intel Xeon cores, 7.0 GB RAM, and 1680 GB local HDD storage.
tagline: A legacy compute instance featuring dedicated physical cores, substantial local HDD storage, and basic processing capabilities for older workloads.
bullet_points: 8 dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 physical cores at 1.8 GHz
7.0 GB of system memory with 1680 GB of local HDD storage
Xen hypervisor virtualization with 1 Gbps baseline network speed
Hardware AES cryptography support but lacks AVX and AVX-512 instructions
Best suited for legacy batch processing and basic database operations
